behaviors.description: The embedding vector produced for a given query string has the correct dimensionality for the vector database index.
behaviors.justification: A mock embedding model could always return vectors of the expected dimension, masking a real mismatch between the embedding model's output and the DB index. To catch this failure, you need to exercise the real embedding model (or one that faithfully reproduces its behavior) and verify dimensions against the actual vector DB schema. A Unit test with a mocked embedding model would pass even if the real embedding API returns a different dimension than expected.
behaviors.test_description: Call the embedding model with sample queries of varying lengths. Retrieve the vector DB index configuration (expected dimension). For each returned embedding, assert that its dimensionality matches the DB schema. Test with multiple embedding models and different query lengths to catch configuration mismatches. Verify the dimension is consistent across repeated calls.

behaviors.description: The similarity search returns results ordered by descending cosine similarity score.
behaviors.justification: A mock vector DB that always returns pre-ordered results would hide the real failure: either the DB returns unordered results, or the code scrambles the order during response formatting. To catch this, you need real DB interaction with known embeddings and similarity scores. A Unit test with a cooperative mock would pass even if the production DB returns unordered results that the code fails to sort.
behaviors.test_description: Insert documents with known embeddings into the vector DB such that similarity scores are non-uniform (e.g., 0.95, 0.72, 0.58). Query with a test embedding. Assert results are ordered by descending similarity score. Test edge cases: tied similarity scores, single result, empty result set. Verify ordering is preserved through the full API response pipeline.

behaviors.description: The LLM re-ranking step changes the order of at least some results compared to the raw similarity ranking when the query has nuanced intent.
behaviors.justification: A mock LLM that always returns a fixed re-ranking would pass this test without verifying the real LLM actually understands nuance or reorders results appropriately. The result depends on what the real LLM produces—whether it truly recognizes contextual intent and adjusts rankings accordingly. Only a real LLM call can verify this behavior. Mocking away the LLM hides the failure mode where the production LLM doesn't actually re-rank nuanced queries.
behaviors.test_description: Create test cases with nuanced intent (e.g., 'best budget laptop for gaming' vs. simple product name queries). For each test case, provide initial ranked results from similarity search and a query. Call the real LLM re-ranking function. Assert the returned ranking differs from the input ranking in at least some positions. Test with multiple nuance levels: simple (single-intent) queries should produce minimal re-ranking, complex queries should produce significant changes. Collect LLM justifications to verify reasoning.

behaviors.description: A query with no close matches in the database returns an empty result set rather than low-relevance noise.
behaviors.justification: A mock vector DB that returns empty for any low-similarity query would pass without verifying the code actually applies a similarity threshold correctly. Real DB behavior is to return results regardless of relevance scores—the code must filter them. Testing with a real vector DB populated with documents and an out-of-domain query is needed to catch the failure mode where low-relevance results (e.g., 0.3 similarity) leak through because the threshold logic is wrong or misconfigured.
behaviors.test_description: Insert a corpus of documents with known domains into the vector DB. Query with an embedding from a completely different domain (e.g., if DB contains 'cooking recipes', query with 'quantum physics' vector). Configure the similarity threshold to a realistic value (e.g., 0.5). Assert the API returns an empty result set. Test multiple threshold values and verify the boundary behavior. Verify that with a very low threshold, the same query returns low-relevance results (confirming the DB has them).

behaviors.description: The end-to-end latency from receiving the query to returning re-ranked results stays below 2 seconds for the median case.
behaviors.justification: Mocking all I/O components (embedding model, vector DB, LLM) with zero latency would always pass, hiding real failures caused by slow external services or inefficient code. Real latency depends on actual network calls, API response times, and LLM inference time. To catch the failure 'system exceeds 2-second SLA under real load', you must measure end-to-end latency with real I/O from embedding, vector DB, and LLM services. Unit or Integration tests with mocked I/O cannot detect this.
behaviors.test_description: Run the API end-to-end with real embedding model, vector DB, and LLM re-ranking services. Measure elapsed time from query submission to response received. Execute at least 20–30 runs with varied query complexity and result set sizes. Calculate P50 (median) latency. Assert P50 < 2 seconds. Separately measure latency contribution of each component (embedding call, DB search, LLM re-ranking, response serialization) to identify bottlenecks. Monitor for outliers and tail latencies (P95, P99).
